content:
The Precision Concepts video series, this is the last in the series. In the first series volume, we talked
about weekly order flow, and in the second volume, we talked about weekly order blocks. Now to bring this
series to a conclusion, I'm looking at the concept of trading inside the range. It's just a concept that I used
to teach my kids how to trade with it. I always try to find a way to conceptualize what it is that I see in the
marketplace. Because I don't have another source readily available so I can just take them to and say this
is what it is that I'm referring to, I kind of dubbed it as an L7 range.

section_title: THE L7 RANGE CONCEPT AND VOLATILITY
content:
What is an L7 range exactly? Whenever you're looking at a price chart, I have purposely picked the US
Swissy pair. It's because I believe this is the king of volatility in terms of pairs. If you were to look at any one
of these reaction high and reaction low, this is a daily chart of the Swissy. I want to draw your attention to a
few things and I want to show you that if directional premise is a stumbling block for you, it need not be.
Because it is not necessary for you to have a directional premise 100 percent of the time.

If you're comfortable trading inside of dealing ranges, it's quite lucrative actually, if you understand what
specifically you're doing when you trade inside of a predetermined range. The reason why I dubbed this as
the L7 Inside the Range concept is because if we look at say, for instance, this range here, this low to this
high, and we'll define it by this. This is a rather simple concept, but it's absolutely amazing if you apply it in
your trading. And actually, if you start looking at it as a training exercise, trade within a predefined range.
Our understanding is that the markets are 70% of the time in a trading range. The definition of that was one
of the longest stumbling blocks for me as a trader when I was first developing, because I always
misunderstood what they were saying. I assumed this was the trading range here, but I looked at this and
say, okay, well, this is a trading range. And look how much time it's spent out of there, and it rallied up here.
To me, this seems like it's always trending and spent very little time inside of a trading range.

It wasn't until I understood the natural ebb and flow in the marketplace where it became quickly apparent
as to what that statement means when markets are 70 percent of the time inside of a trading range. We
have a high here and a low here. The range is defined by this low and this high. The likelihood of price
moving out and continuously to move higher is rather low. In other words, the way I interpret it, and you can
go with this if you want, I view this as we have 70% odds that this market is going to have a very difficult
time breaking out to run to new highs. It could stab through. The way I interpret that is that as the market
begins to move outside of that range high, that statement indicates that it has a 70% chance of failure to
move to new highs and continue higher. Yes, we see a violation on a few candles on the daily chart where it
did in fact sweep above that range high back here, but ultimately what did it do? It returned back inside of
the defined range of this low and this high back here.

section_title: APPLYING THE L CONCEPT WITH ORDER BLOCKS
content:
How can we use this information and what does it mean to us as a trader? As price trades down and
makes a low here, I'll ask you this: what do you think the least road of resistance is? Is it to go higher or to
go lower? Because we've already went lower than this low here, we went lower than this low here. We took
out this high with a very small minor move here, but we've recently started to move higher. If you start
looking at this like in a letter L, and envision a letter L here, we're trading down here at these levels here.
Where is the point of reference for this range? If we're at the lows here, it's up here. So now, how do we
incorporate that? At this old high, if we start looking at the order blocks that represent the selling that
caused this market to drop, we will look at a few of them. We have this is a Sunday, so I'm going to avoid
that. We're going to say this is the first bullish candle prior to this Sunday here. And if we're down here, we
could look to see if price would rally back up to that price point. Yes, invariably we could probably see it
retest this high, but what do we do? We already state that markets have a 70% likelihood of staying inside
of predetermined ranges. So if we could find a long down in here, we could look for this market to move
higher up into this level here. Also note that we have this shorter term range from this high and all that was
note the bullish candle prior to dropping in this low here. So this is a predetermined range as well. If we
were long here, we could look for initial objectives in here. And if this high is taken out, reasonably expect
this order block here to be retested, and if it continues to contain even higher, we could retest this high here
and maybe look to see some kind of a sweeping event that I've mentioned many times in my videos or in
commentary.

section_title: TRADING COUNTER ORDER BLOCKS AND RANGE EXTREMES
content:
As the market also traded in this range with this high here and this low, the range was this high and this
low. If we could find a long in here, we could trade inside the range and look for this bearish ICT order block
to be retested. We entered into that same area, had a small little turtle soup, which is a false break above
old high and then rejection. But I'm not trying to teach that pattern here, so I probably shouldn't have said
that. But any long in here, your objective would be to look for the counter order block that saw price drop
down. We would try to trade back up into it. This would be a known level of institutional selling. You can see
that does in fact come under way. The market does drop down precipitously.

section_title: UNDERSTANDING THE 'L7' RANGE AND PRICE FAILURE
content:
We're using the L concept in here, this particular approach. It's probably an oversimplification, but to me it
just makes sense and it helps my kids learn this as I teach it to them. So we have a point of where we are
now and where do we go from here? Well, the range high is here, so that would form a letter L. And it
probably sounds like Sesame Street to people in the states, but it really is a rather simple approach. Now,
how do we approach an L7 range? Or the concept, how does the seven come into play? Well, if you look
at, and we get some of these lines off here because it's going to get in my way here. Price at these levels,
and we're not going to reference anything over here for now. We're just going to look at where we're at in
terms of the dealing ranges and such. We have this high here and you see price run off back into the order
block here and then start to sell off. If we were able to see something bearish back here, the point of
reference is what? Here's the area where we're at. The likelihood of us continuously breaking higher,
because this was the previous high, price rallied out of that. Now it's several hundred pips because it is a
daily chart, but look what happened: it was unable to stay above this range high, so it pulled back inside of
the range to run below this old low. But more importantly, it's this range low here and the new high that it
formed.

section_title: IDENTIFYING MULTIPLE DEALING RANGES AND PATH OF LEAST RESISTANCE
content:
We have the dealing ranges, this high, this low. So if you're up here hunting sell signals, where's the range?
Well, we have several ranges to deal with. So that's the question and why I always ask, 'where are we
inside the dealing with what dealing range are we within?' We have these highs, we have a reaction low
here, so that's a point of reference. This is one point of reference, this is another point of reference, another
point of reference, and another point of reference. So these are all ranges that price works within while
trading. This low here, price bounced off it, it's pretty nice. Obviously, price came down into this low here,
swept up just a little bit, and then rallied even more.

Price when we made this range here, we have this high and this low. Where's the path of least resistance?
It's going to be on the upside because it's been dropping lower, lower, lower, lower, lower. And now the only
thing that we got to consider is where the stops are, and that's where the price is going to seek. It's going to
seek liquidity because everything's been going lower, so eventually there's going to be a low and selling.
So what will happen is, tomorrow, it'll reach up and seek out liquidity above the short term highs and see if
there's any stops. But still notice, even as aggressive as this bounce was and rally higher, where does it run
back to? Previous order block, this bullish candle prior to the drop lower. If you want to use this candle as
well, you're still within that same range right here. So inside this range we've come all the way back up to
here. So now again, while price is right here, is it likely to continue and break through this high and trade on
to new highs? Odds tell us statistically that 70% of the time price will stay inside of a range. So as price
rallies on up here aggressively, when we start moving back into these dual bearish order blocks, it's
reasonable to expect this market to try to look for sell scenarios or sell patterns. Now, where's the range at
this point? Because we have this high here and this low where it came from originally down here. So the
order block that resides down here is this one. So if we sell up in here, we could reasonably expect it to
come back down into this area and maybe retest this low. But again, we have 70% odds that it's going to
stay within this range.

section_title: RANGE DYNAMICS, LIQUIDITY SWEEPS, AND ORDER BLOCK RETESTS
content:
Price comes down, does it break this low? No, what does it do? It rallies once more. How why is it rallying
up here? We have a high here, we have a short term high here. This is a bearish order block with trades
back into. This is a bearish order block that it trades back into. The range is this short term high
- this short
term low. Notice we did not classify it as this low because this is the true range here. This is the range that
has not been violated, this high and this low. So as we have this short term high, price moves from this
point down, does not break this low and rallies back up. Where does it rally up to? Above the short term
highs, taking out any liquidity that's resting in the marketplace, this short term high here and this short term
high here. Both being order blocks that it does trade within, and then what does it do? It sells back off. Now
we do see price violate this low and the old reference point low here. It breaks below it, it sweeps back into
the range here. Now what's going on here? Well, we have a new range. We have this high here and we
have a new range low here. So we have this high to this low. Price rallies on up and where's it go back to?
A previous bearish order block right here, this candle right there. Draw that out in time, you see it tag it right
there. So now what are we saying here? What's the premise behind this?

If you understand where we are in terms of the present trading range, you don't need a directional premise.
You can trade very very short term, either intraday or day trading or short term trading, with a premise in
mind that as long as you know you're not going to overstay your welcome, you have the likelihood of taking
short term intraday scalps, short term intraday price swings, and short term trading and capture a lot of
these little little moves here. Now if you're very disciplined, you can do this over and over and over again
with a good pair like this. Like I said, the Swissy is pretty volatile and this gives you a lot of opportunities to
trade like this. I don't trade the Swissy a lot, but if I do trade it, I trade it in this capacity because I don't feel
that it's one of the pairs that I can trust because it's very very volatile. It comes down a lot, takes out lows,
takes out highs and its characteristic is very, it's very range bound.

You can trade a lot of setups with this pair. You have a bullish order block here and price dips down into,
rallies. And you can take in here, yes it violates it, it goes through it, but look at the profit potential that's
available by understanding where you are in the range, that's 280 pips catching a buy like that. Just so you
know, I'm not cherry-picking, we have a dealing range low here and a dealing range high here. Price rallies
back up to what? The old order block right here, sells off. Where does it go? Down to this range high to this
range low, to the previous bullish order block right here. Boom, buy, take profits at old highs. Sure you can
hold on to higher prices, but there's no guarantee it's going to do it. Remember, we have a 70% chance
that price will stay within a range. So you can take a large portion of your trade off, maybe 80%, leave 20%
on these types of incidents. That's what we're talking about when we talked about taking partial portions of
our trade off. You want to be doing it with a systematic approach to it, but a framework as to why you're
doing it. Because as we trade up to here, there's no guarantee this is going to continue going up. It could
deeply retrace and you'll have to ride through all that, and many times it could just continue to trade lower
and blow out your stops. So it's nicer as a trader to take some profits as a logical place of resistance where
we could see potentially a retracement where we don't want to ride through that. Because if we're at the
extreme of the range, we want to hopefully see this continuation, but there's no guarantee it's going to
happen. So by having that understanding, we're trading with a 70% chance of yes, this may hold us lower.

You're taking a 100% likelihood of taking a large portion of your trade off. So let's say you took 80% off at
this high from a low down here, and you went long on a previous order block. You're trading inside the
range here, this is your high and this is your low. We know that 70% of the time, we're 70% odds that you
are not going to see a higher breakout. That means take a larger portion your trade off. So let's say we took
80% off from a long down here, leave 20% on, and look what it does: you end up making more money on
the 20% remaining in the position than you did on your 80% taking it off from here to here. And you're
keeping risk low, you're minimizing risk and maximizing profit potential. Now we have another range here,
we have a low and a high. So this high and this low, the order block is right here, dips down into it. 70%
likelihood that this range low will maintain and this range high will maintain. So what do we do? You can be
a buyer here and taking profits here. If that's the case and we buy right at the order block, high, that's 343
pips made available. And taking out again, 80% of the position right here, leaving 20% on and look what it
does, it gives you a little bit more of a pump in that position.

section_title: ADDRESSING LOSSES AND USING FIBONACCI FOR OPTIMAL ENTRY
content:
Now we have another dealing range, we have this low and this high. Price comes down, and here is your
bullish order block right in here. This right here would have amounted to a loss. I want to give you
opportunities where this would not work. I don't want to sit here and look at a chart and cherry-pick
everything in hindsight and say, 'look how great I am' or 'look at my creative tools are', but this is one where
if you were trying to capitalize on this range phenomenon, it didn't work. But I'm going to ask you a
question: if you use your fibs in conjunction with this, wouldn't this be below the 79% retracement level? So
would this swing qualify up here? It would, but you didn't dip down into the order block far enough back
here.

AVOIDANCE
Forgive me, I can't remember if I went over this pattern here, this barrier block here, but we have one. Price
breaks back down below inside this dealing range high here, and we violated this low here. We have a new
range low and this range high up here. But inside of this range, we have this high, this high, this high, this
high, this high, this high. So each one of these are potential dealing ranges. The way we use it, and it's put
them on the charts, you can see it. I'll leave this one alone because it's a Sunday, I'm sure. So we have this
low down here which this uses a full horizontal for that. So we have this range and we have this range, and
we have this range and we have the old high back here and the low here prior to this rally up. Let's
incorporate our fib. Price retraces to, well, this level here absolutely means nothing to me. So I wouldn't, I
wouldn't look at this Fib level as a means of interest. I like the 62 from this high here. We still don't have
anything from this high here, we have nothing. Still, we have this high here and suddenly we have a sweet
spot. Now what's nice about this one is we have old lows here. Actually, we have a nice fractal low that was
broken to the downside and we came back and retested it. See how prices moved back into this area here?
See this low, lower low, higher low? So we have a fractal here, we broke through it and then retested it in
here, back in this area over here. But now look behind price, what do you see in this consolidation in here?
What do you see where's the order block at? So yeah, right there. So inside this range, we've rallied back
up. I would not, this is, and this is where we're getting at with this. I would not be willing to assume new long
positions trading up against this bearish order block and on a daily chart, and we're at the extreme of the
range. Again, we're getting up to these levels here and let me borrow this level, here's that swing high. So
dealing range high is here, we're off the lows here. We got a 70% likelihood this is going to maintain price.
That's what statistics tell us. Price spends 70% of time inside of a range. So as we get up into this level,
look what we're doing. Would you be willing to buy up here up against a bearish order block at the far
extreme of a dealing range on a daily chart like this? So inside this range, we have very little to go by in
terms of statistical odds. In fact, it's statistically against us. The odds are that this is not likely to continue
higher. And that's the benefit of looking at where we are in a current range.

section_title: HIGH PROBABILITY TRADING AT RANGE EXTREMES
content:
Now let's go back to this level over here. Down in here, we have a bullish order block, and very handsome,
you can see how that was handled there. As price starts to break down, notice how we got very very very
close to it but never violated it. So price stayed what, inside the range. Price eventually comes down to that
range, breaks it a little bit, but look what it does: it trades deep back into the range again, sweeps below it
again, works at a couple times, but then eventually moves outside of the range. But look how long it took to
get outside of that range, a very long time going back through all of these levels. We can see many
instances where trading by defining where we are in terms of the current trading range, you could trade
counter swing trades both directions. Like this level in here, you have a bearish order block here, you have
a bearish order block here. And I apologize if this seems like a whole lot of just looking at a chart and
grabbing the best positions. I promise you if you spend a week going through every pair that you'd like to
trade and go through and look at highs and lows, old highs and lows and see where prices retraced and all
of the retraces back to the highs and lows, you'll see that there are absolutely beautiful trading
opportunities where over and over and over again you could be really finding a lot of times what many
traders can't see on the chart. This low to this high, really handsome opportunity again. Now once we get
up here, what do we do? We can look for a bearish scenario. If you're a harmonic trader, you look for
bearish bats, bearish butterflies, bearish Gartleys up in here. There's turtle soups, bearish stochastic,
bearish MACD divergence, Head and Shoulders formations, all those types of patterns in this area here.
Trade looking for what? The retracement back into the dealing range here, and this is your bullish order
block here, and we would look to take an exit point and maybe even look for a bullish pattern to trade in
here with the expectation of this high being retested. So it gives you a means of trading at the very very
extreme of each range. So when you're looking at the chart, you want to be looking at doing at least 90
percent of your trades at the far extreme of each range that you're working within. The likelihood of trading
in the middle of the range is much much lower if you trade in the middle range versus the extreme outer
range. Like up in here, you being a seller up here versus being a buyer, it goes without saying, it's pretty
obvious. Price has rallied this far, just like we've seen here, price rallies in this far one direction, you're
going to the outer cusp, the outer range of this trading range. The likelihood of it continuing up is far far less
than the likelihood it going lower.

section_title: PATH OF LEAST RESISTANCE AND TRADE FREQUENCY
content:
Again, the question is, where are we in the range and where is the path of least resistance? And if you
think like that, look into the charts, defining the current trading range that we're working within and
understanding the mechanics are 70% of time price stays in a range. It does not mean 100% of time is
going to be like that, but notice what we're doing: we're looking for trades back and forth each direction and
not having to rely on a trend directional bias like we talked about in the first two volumes. And the reason
why I'm including this is because I know some of you simply can't trust the moving averages and you want
to have something that has a lot more action in your trading. If you start trading inside the range like this,
you're going to have more trading than you ever dreamed of, but you're also going to have the likelihood of
losing more times than you probably would if you just stayed on one side of the marketplace like we
discussed on volumes 1 & 2.
CONCLUSION:

section_title: MASTERFUL RANGE TRADING FOR COMPOUNDING EQUITY
content:
So to recap, when we look at price, we want to find out again, where are we at in the range? What dealing
range highs and lows are we working within? And the likelihood of that range staying intact is 70%. That's
pretty good statistical odds. If you add that equation with the order blocks, and you keep your profits very
concise, very objective. In other words, this is an area where you would expect to see selling because we
had selling all through here, but more importantly, we have a very clear bearish order block. Price when
trapping at a level, and what happened, true to form, it dropped down. Look at this, it goes right back into a
previous bullish order block. There's a perfect opportunity to take your profits just doing a trade like that.
Just simply doing something like that here to here. How many pips would it be? It would be too far for me to
say that realistically there's a good 60 pips in that that you could have taken out. Think about that, it's not
getting every high and low, it's about getting portions of these moves and banking them and compounding
the growth of your equity over a period of time. And I promise you if you spend time looking at the charts
and studying it, you'll see many many more opportunities trading inside the range.
